coredata.dc:title: Bimetallic gold/silver nanoclusters-gold nanoparticles based fluorescent sensing platform via the inner filter effect for hyaluronidase activity detection
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Qing
Yan, Xu
Lai, Qi
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: Bimetallic nanoclusters, compared to monometallic nanoclusters, possess better chemical performance. Herein, with the assistance of chondroitin sulfate, a novel fluorescent gold/silver nanoclusters (Au/Ag NCs) have been developed and applied to establish a sensing platform for sensitive detection of hyaluronidase (HAase). The Au/Ag NCs fluorescence intensity can be distinctly quenched by gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) due to the inner-filter effect (IFE). With the introduction of protamine (PRO), the positively charged PRO can bind to negatively charged Au NPs via electrostatic attraction resulting in the aggregation of gold nanoparticles. When PRO was preferential interaction with the negatively charged hyaluronic acid (HA), it would lead to the disaggregation of Au NPs and quench the ﬂuorescence again. However, HAase can hydrolyze HA into small fragments which prevented HA interacting with protamine, and leading to the aggregation of Au NPs and weaken the IFE of Au NPs on Au/Ag NCs. Thus, the ﬂuorescence of bimetallic nanoclusters was “turn-on”. This “turn-on” sensing system can be utilized to detect HAase level in the range of 0.5–37.5 U mL−1. The developed strategy not only expands the application scope of bimetallic nanoclusters, but also provides a new approach for the determination of HAase.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bimetallic gold/silver nanoclusters
Hyaluronidase
Gold nanoparticles
Inner-filter effect

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: HAase activity assay in serum samples
Synthesis and characterization of bimetallic nanoclusters
The detection strategy of HAase activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of sensing strategy for monitoring HAase activity based on electrostatic attraction and IFE.

(a) The effect of pH on the FL intensity of Au/Ag NCs. (b) The effect of NaCl concentration on the FL intensity of Au/Ag NCs. (c) The effect of temperature on the FL intensity of Au/Ag NCs. (d) The photobleaching experiment of the Au/Ag NCs.

(a) The TEM image of Au/Ag NCs. Inset showed high-resolution TEM; (b) The size distribution of Au/Ag NCs; (c) FT-IR spectrum of Au/Ag NCs (black line) and CS (red line). (d) The UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line) and the fluorescence excitation (blue line) and emission (red line) spectra of Au/Ag NCs (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) The fluorescence spectra of Au/Ag NCs (black curve), Au/Ag NCs + Au NPs (red curve), Au/Ag NCs + Au NPs + PRO (blue curve); Au/Ag NCs + Au NPs + PRO+HA (pink curve); Au/Ag NCs + Au NPs + PRO+HA+HAase (green curve); (b) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Au NPs (black line) and fluorescence excitation of Au/Ag NCs (red line); (c) The zeta potential of Au/Ag NCs, Au NPs, PRO and HA. (d) Fluorescence lifetime of Au/Ag NCs (black line) and Au/Ag NCs + Au NPs (red line) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) The fluorescence spectra of Au/Ag NCs/Au NPs/PRO/HA system towards different levels of HAase. The levels of HAase from bottom to up are 0, 0.5, 5, 8, 12, 15, 18, 25, 30 and 37.5 U mL−1, respectively. Inset shows the linearity of the linear relationship of relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) versus HAase concentration. I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of Au/Ag NCs/Au NPs/PRO/HA system before and after the incorporation of HAase, respectively. (b) The selectivity of the Au/Ag NCs/Au NPs/PRO/HA system for detection of HAase over coexisting substances. (1 mmol L−1 NaCl, KCl, glucose, 100 μmol L−1 phenylalanine, threonine, alanine, 0.3 g L−1 urease, trypsin, pepsin).
Determination of HAase in Human Serum Samples.

head.ce:title: Bimetallic gold/silver nanoclusters-gold nanoparticles based fluorescent sensing platform via the inner filter effect for hyaluronidase activity detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, College of Electron Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics
College of Electron Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bimetallic nanoclusters, compared to monometallic nanoclusters, possess better chemical performance. Herein, with the assistance of chondroitin sulfate, a novel fluorescent gold/silver nanoclusters (Au/Ag NCs) have been developed and applied to establish a sensing platform for sensitive detection of hyaluronidase (HAase). The Au/Ag NCs fluorescence intensity can be distinctly quenched by gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) due to the inner-filter effect (IFE). With the introduction of protamine (PRO), the positively charged PRO can bind to negatively charged Au NPs via electrostatic attraction resulting in the aggregation of gold nanoparticles. When PRO was preferential interaction with the negatively charged hyaluronic acid (HA), it would lead to the disaggregation of Au NPs and quench the ﬂuorescence again. However, HAase can hydrolyze HA into small fragments which prevented HA interacting with protamine, and leading to the aggregation of Au NPs and weaken the IFE of Au NPs on Au/Ag NCs. Thus, the ﬂuorescence of bimetallic nanoclusters was “turn-on”. This “turn-on” sensing system can be utilized to detect HAase level in the range of 0.5–37.5 U mL−1. The developed strategy not only expands the application scope of bimetallic nanoclusters, but also provides a new approach for the determination of HAase.

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of promising nanomaterials in recent years, metal nanoclusters (NCs) have sparked wide-spread attention. The diameter of metal NCs is smaller than 2 nm, which is equivalent to the Fermi wavelength of electrons between plasmonic metal nanoparticles and metal atoms [1]. The special size effect makes the metal NCS possess the molecule-like characteristics such as size-dependent fluorescence and discrete energy levels. [2]. Due to their excellent features, for example chemical stability, good biocompatibility and photostability, low toxicity and broad spectral emission from near infrared to visible region [3], metal NCs show great potential in many aspects of biochemical applications including sensing [4–6], living cells imaging [7,8], and disease diagnosis and treatment [9,10]. Nowadays, more and more efforts have been devoted to preparation of bimetallic NCs, which possess conspicuous advantages compared with the monometallic ones for physical, chemical properties and catalytic performance [11–13]. Recently, Su’s group [14] has successfully fabricated novel ﬂuorescent DNA-templated Cu/Ag NCs through a simple approach. The optical signal of the DNA-Ag NCs can be enhanced 4.5-fold by introduction of copper (Cu) ions forming DNA-Cu/Ag NCs. However, salt induced instability greatly limited its application [15]. Furthermore, several bimetallic Au/Ag NCs have been developed, aiming to combine “silver effect” to change the luminescence of gold nanoclusters. For example, strong fluorescent Au/Ag NCs were synthesized by Liu and co-workers in the protein matrix [16]. Huang’s group has designed and prepared the lipoic acid-capped Au/Ag NCs with strong red-emitting ﬂuorescence [17]. Although many bimetallic metal NCs have been exploited as probes for detection of ions and some biomolecules, such as Cu2+ [16], Al3+ [18], GSH [19] and so on, the research and application of bimetallic metal NCs for sensing biological enzyme are still in the initial stage.

Hyaluronidase (HAase) is a family of endoglycosidases that can degrade the N-acetyl-d-glucosaminidic linkages in the hyaluronic acid (HA) [20]. It has been reported that HAase is involved in many physiological and pathological processes, including fertilization, embryogenesis, maintenance of the elastoviscosity in tissues, inflammation, and wound healing [21,22]. Recently, an abnormal expression of HAase has been found be relevant to many malignant tumors like prostate cancers [23] and bladder cancers [24]. Therefore, to develop a facile and accurate strategy for the detection of the HAase level in serving tumor diagnosis and therapy is important and desired. Many traditional methods have also been reported for monitoring HAase, including turbidimetry [25], viscosimetry [26], immunoassay [27] and zymography [28]. However, there are still some drawbacks to be improved of the mentioned approaches, such as the necessity of the expensive antibodies and reagents and lack of sensitivity and selectivity [29]. Nowadays, fluorescence method has been received more and more researchers’ attention due to its remarkable characteristics of simplicity and high sensitivity, and several novel fluorescent biosensor have been reported for HAase assay. For instance, Xie’s group [30] has developed a novel fluorescent sensing system for HAase detection relied on electrostatic interactions between two positively charged anthracene and tetraphenylethylene derivatives and the negatively charged HA. However, the procedures of these well-designed ﬂuorophore synthesis and puriﬁcation are tedious and time consuming [31]. Gu et al. used molybdenum disulﬁde quantum dots (MoS2 QDs) as fluorescence probes for HAase detection via photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between the fluorophore and HA-functionalized AuNPs (HA-AuNPs) [32]. Yang et al. designed a fluorescent platform for sensing hyaluronic acid and HAase on the basis of electrostatic adsorption of carbon dots (CDs) towards HA [33]. Both the MoS2 QDs and CDs as signal reporters exhibit blue-emitting fluorescence, which are easy to be disturbed by some protein emission limiting their further application in the biological system.

In this work, a new bimetallic Au/Ag NCs was successfully synthesized using chondroitin sulfate as template. By combining of Au/Ag NCs and gold nanoparticles (Au NPs), we constructed a fluorescence sensing platform for HAase detection. The detection strategy was displayed in Scheme 1 . HA, a negatively charged linear mucopolysaccharide, was preferential interaction with protamine by electrostatic attraction. In presence of HAase, HA can be cut into fragments which can’t interact with protamine (PRO), leading to the aggregation of Au NPs. Dispersed Au NPs can lead to the signiﬁcant quenching of Au/Ag NCs fluorescence intensity via an efﬁcient inner ﬁlter effect (IFE). The aggregation of Au NPs caused by PRO would restrict the IFE of dispersed Au NPs on Au/Ag NCs and restore the fluorescence signal of bimetallic nanoclusters accordingly [34]. Thus, the ﬂuorescence of Au/Ag NCs quenched by dispersed Au NPs was recovered. This “turn-on” fluorescent strategy could reduce the background noise, and avoid the interference of some proteins. Notably, the enzyme biosensor based on bimetallic Au/Ag NCs and Au NPs for HAase assay has not been discovered before.

Chondroitin sulfate (CS), dopamine, protamine, hyaluronic acid (HA) and amino acid were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin biochemical Co. Ltd. HAase, trypsin, pyrophosphatase, lysozyme tyrosinase and urease were got from Sigma-Aldrich. Other analytically pure of inorganic chemicals were obtained from Beijing Chemical Co Beijing Dingguo Biotechnology Co. Ltd. The water applied in throughout experiments had a good resistivity (>18 MΩ cm−1).

Fluorescence spectra were performed with a RF-5301 PC spectrofluorophotometer. FT-IR spectra were carried out by 16 Bruker IFS66 V FT-IR spectrometer. Fluorescence decays and quantum yield were recorded by FLS920 Edinburgh Fluorescence Lifetime and Steady State Spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were made with a Varian GBC Cintra 10e UV–vis Spectrophotometer. TEM images were measured by a Hitachi H-800 transmission electron microscope.

Briefly, 100 μL AgNO3 (67.5 mmol L−1) and 188 μL HAuCl4 (0.01 g mL−1) was introduced to the 2.5 mL CS solution (10 mg mL−1) under vigorous stirring. After 30 min later, 240 μL 20 mmol L-1 dopamine was added into the system. After stirring at 30℃ for 4 h, the color of the solution turned to reddish brown illustrating Au/Ag NCs have been successfully prepared. The Au/Ag NCs solution was purified with passing through 0.22 μm filters and stored at refrigerator 4 °C before used.

According to the previous protocol, the Au NPs was synthesized by the reduction of HAuCl4 with sodium citrate [35]. In brief, 75 mL H2O solution accompanied with 0.75 mL HAuCl4·4H2O (1%) were added into a 100 mL round flask, and the mixture was heated to reflux under vigorous stirring. Subsequently, 3.75 mL sodium citrate solution (1%) was quickly added into system. After refluxed for an additional 5 min, the color of the mixture was converted from pale yellow to deep wine red to obtain monodispersed Au NPs. According to Lambert Beer's law, the molar concentration of the Au NPs was calculated to be about 2.8 nmol L−1 (the molar extinction coefficient (ε) for the Au NPs = 2.7 × 108 mol-1 cm−1 [36]). The Au NPs solution was kept at 4 °C when it was not utilized.

For HAase activity detection, 100 μL HA (0.05%), 50 μL Tris−HCl (1.0 mmol L−1, pH = 7.4) and 50 μL different concentrations of HAase were incubated at 37 °C in 1.5 mL calibrated test tubes. After 60 min, 25 μL protamine (2 mg/mL) and 125 μL H2O were added to the system. 5 min later, 30 μL Au NPs was introduced. Last, 20 μL Au/Ag NCs was added into solution and the 400 μL final volume of detection system was obtained. After mixing evenly, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded in the range of 470 nm–750 nm at the excitation wavelength of 450 nm. All spectral testing experiments were carried out at room temperature (298 K).

ce:section.ce:section-title: HAase activity assay in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, human blood samples required for the detection of the actual samples were obtained from Changchun China Japan United hospital. First, the blood samples were kept for one hour without shaking. Then, centrifuges were applied to centrifugate blood samples at 10,000 rpm (6820 g) for 10 min to remove interfering substances such as erythrocytes. Finally, the supernatant was diluted 10 times with deionized water, and several spiked serum samples were prepared by addition different concentrations of HAase. For HAase determination in real sample, the pure HAase was replaced with the equal volume of above-mentioned serum sample, and other detection procedures were performed in compliance with the part of 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of bimetallic nanoclusters
ce:section.ce:para: The CS-capped bimetallic nanoclusters were successfully synthesized by using HAuCl4·4H2O, AgNO3 and CS as the raw materials via one-pot reaction. In order to achieve better performance, some parameters in the process of synthesis, including the molar ratios of HAuCl4·4H2O and AgNO3, reaction time and reaction temperature were investigated. From Fig. S1a, we could find that the FL intensity of Au/Ag NCs initial significantly enhanced with the increasing of AgNO3 concentrations. These phenomena could illustrate that the addition of Ag could increase the luminescence intensity of the Au NCs, which was probably attributed to Au and Ag have a synergistic effect in the formation of nanoclusters [37]. When the molar ratio of Ag/Au precursors was raised to 1.5:1, the fluorescence signal of bimetallic nanoclusters reached the maximum. Then, the effect of reaction time was optimized. From Fig. S1b, the fluorescence signal of bimetallic nanoclusters was gradually increased with prolongation of incubation time and the strongest fluorescence intensity was obtained at 4 h. In addition, the reaction temperature was also studied in Fig. S1c. It can be observed that with the increase of temperature, the fluorescence intensity was gradually increased. When the temperature is over 30 °C and continues to rise, the fluorescence intensity evidently decreased. Hence, we chose the molar ratio of Ag and Au precursors 1.5:1, temperature of 30 °C and reaction time of 4 h as the optimal condition in the synthesis experiments.

In order to demonstrate the obtained Au/Ag NCs have potential practical value, the chemical stability and photostability of bimetallic nanoclusters were also investigated in this study. As depicted in Fig. 1 a, the FL intensity was slowly decreased with the increasing of pH values from 5.0 to 10.0, implying the better luminescence performance in weak acid and neutral environment. Fig. 1 b showed the effect of salt concentration on the stability of nanoclusters. It could be found that the fluorescence properties of the prepared nanoclusters remained almost constant even at higher salt concentration. As can be seen from Fig. 1c, the FL intensity was relatively stable in the temperature range of 20–40 °C. When the temperature was higher than 40 °C, the FL intensity of nanoclusters would be decreased. Taking into consideration of photostability, the nanoclusters solution was irradiated continuously by 250 W xenon lamp over a period of 120 min and the photobleaching curve of Au/Ag NCs was shown in Fig. 1 d. These results showed that the fluorescence intensity of bimetallic nanoclusters preserved ∼80% of the initial intensity after 120 min of lamp irradiation, which indicated the Au/Ag NCs exhibited a high photostability. Inspired by the above situations, the as-prepared Au/Ag NCs shows good stability, making them as competitive candidates for biosensing applications.

The morphology of Au/Ag NCs was characterized by TEM image. As described in Fig. 2 a, the Au/Ag NCs was near spherical with an average diameter of 0.99 nm, and had good dispersion (Fig. 2b). FT-IR spectra was applied for investigation functional groups on the surface of nanomaterial (Fig. 2c). Peaks at about 3400 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 respectively belonged to strong characteristic stretching and deformation vibration of COH. Peaks at 2800–2950 cm−1 were ascribed for C–H stretching vibrations. Peak at 1620 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CO. And the band at around 1230 cm-1 and 1040 cm−1 presented the asymmetric and symmetric vibrational absorption of SO3H groups [38,39]. It can be seen that CO and SO3H of CS was more than that of Au/Ag NCs, suggesting carboxyl and sulfonic groups were involved in the forming of coordination bond with gold and silver ions to generate bimetallic nanoclusters. Moreover, absorption and fluorescence spectra were employed to demonstrate the optical properties of the nanoclusters. As shown in Fig. 2d, localized surface plasmon resonance band of Au NPs did not appear in the visible region. The Au/Ag NCs exhibited the maximum emission at 510 nm with excitation wavelength at 450 nm. Also using the different excitation wavelengths, the fluorescence spectra in the range of 400–470 were recorded in Fig. S2, which indicated Au/Ag NCs had the constant fluorescence emission. Moreover, the fluorescence quantum yield of bimetallic nanoclusters was measured to be 1.41%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection strategy of HAase activity
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 depicted the designed strategy for the ﬂuorescence assay of HAase. Some measurements were employed to confirm the feasibility of the designed sensing principle. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the FL intensity at 510 nm of the Au/Ag NCs (black curve) can be effectively quenched after the addition of Au NPs (red curve, inset 1). From Fig. 3b, it can be seen that the absorption spectrum of Au NPs remarkably overlapped the ﬂuorescence emission of Au/Ag NCs, illustrating that quenching mechanism was probably attributed to the IFE or ﬂuorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Fig. 3 c showed the zeta potential of the citrate-stabilized Au NPs and Au/Ag NCs were −43.27 mV and −32.07 mV, respectively. Because both Au/Ag NCs and Au NPs had negative charges, there was no possibility of electrostatic interaction between the bimetallic nanoclusters and gold nanoparticles. From Fig. 3d, the fluorescence lifetime of Au/Ag NCs was hardly changed with the incorporation of Au NPs, which was a strong evidence to prove that the fluorescence quenching of Au/Ag NCs by Au NPs was ascribed for IFE. When the positively charged PRO (zeta potential of PRO is + 18.65 mV, Fig. 3c) was mixed with negatively charged Au NPs, the electrostatic attraction would cause the aggregation of Au NPs. And the aggregation of Au NPs would restricted the IFE of dispersed Au NPs on Au/Ag NCs and the fluorescence intensity of bimetallic nanoclusters was recovered accompanied with the color of the solution turned from red to blue (Fig. 3 a blue curve, insert 2). More direct evidence for this statement came from the TEM images. Fig. S3a displayed that the Au NPs in TEM image was near spherical and had good dispersibility. And it became aggregated in the presence of PRO (Fig. S3b).

Furthermore, HA exhibits long chain or reticular structure. When HA was added to the system, the positively charged PRO will preferentially interact with the negatively charged HA (zeta potential of HA is −32.23 mV, Fig. 3c) via electrostatic attraction. So, the dispersed Au NPs caused the ﬂuorescence quenching again and the color of solution turned to red (Fig. 3 a pink curve, insert 3). In presence of HAase, HAase can catalyze HA to small fragments, which can’t interact with protamine and lead to aggregation of Au NPs again. Thus, the ﬂuorescence of sensing system was recovered (Fig. 3 a green curve), and the color of solution changed to blue (Fig. 3 a insert 4). In addition, the effects of individually PRO, HA and HAase on the fluorescence of Au/Ag NCs were also studied. From Fig. S4, it was observed that only PRO can slightly enhance the fluorescence, and the ﬂuorescence intensity of Au/Ag NCs had almost no changes in the presence of HA or HAase. Moreover, HA or HAase had no impact on the Au/Ag NCs/Au NPs system fluorescence. All the above-mentioned results showed this sensing strategy is feasible.

Before HAase detection, relevant experimental conditions were investigated in detail. First of all, the IFE of Au NPs on Au/Ag NCs was systematically studied. As can be seen from Fig. S5a, the FL intensity was decreased with the increase concentration of Au NPs from 0 to 0.28 nmol L−1. Moreover, we further investigated the recovery efficiency of PRO for Au/Ag NCs quenched by different concentration of Au NPs. From Fig. S5b, it can be found that the best recovery efficiency of PRO was obtained for Au/Ag NCs quenched by 0.21 nmol L-1 Au NPs. So, 0.21 nmol L-1 was selected as the optimal concentration of Au NPs. Next, we investigated the influence of PRO concentration. As shown in Fig. S6a, with the increasing of PRO concentration from 0 to 125 μg mL−1, the FL intensity of Au/Ag NCs/Au NPs system was gradually recovered. Thus, 125 μg mL-1 PRO was chosen for the following determination.

The concentrations of HA was optimized for the consequently detection of HAase. As shown in Fig. S6b, with the increasing of HA concentration, the FL intensity of Au/Ag NCs/Au NPs/PRO system was gradually decreased. And when the concentration of HA was up to 125 μg mL−1, the FL signal of mixture reached a plateau. So, 125 μg mL−1 of HA was chosen as the optimal substrate concentration for enzyme detection. In addition, pH and enzyme reaction time, NaCl concentration and temperature for HAase detection were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S6c and Fig. S6d, the change of FL intensity of Au/Ag NCs/Au NPs/PRO/HA system was the most significant at pH 7.4 before and after the addition of HAase, and the fluorescence intensity of the system was increased and then kept stable after 60 min. Fig. S6e illustrated that NaCl concentration in the range of 1∼10 mmol L-1 had little impact on the HAase detection. And, 37 ℃ was the optimal reaction temperature for enzyme determination (Fig. S6f).

Having determined the most suitable conditions, the fluorescence spectra of Au/Ag NCs/Au NPs/PRO/HA system towards vairous concentration of HAase were displayed in Fig. 4
a. The fluorescence intensity of the sensing system was gradually enhanced with the increasing level of HAase from 0.5 to 37.5 U mL−1. Inset showed that there was a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0 and concentration of HAase. The regression equation is I/I0 = 1.0053 + 0.0178CHAase, U mL−1 (R2 = 0.9962). The detection limit (LOD) for HAase is 0.3 U mL−1. The linear range and LOD of several previously reported methods and our work were summarized in Table S1. It is found that this proposed sensing platform shows a comparable analytical range and detection limit.

Selectivity is an essential index to assess the applicability of biosensors in a relatively complex biological environment. For a new proposed method, poor selectivity will greatly restrict its application. So, the selectivity experiment for this sensing strategy was carried out by assessing the fluorescence response of sensing system towards different enzymes and other biological substances including Na+, K+, glucose, phenylalanine, threonine, alanine, urease, trypsin, pepsin. From Fig. 4b, we can see that the optical signal of Au/Ag NCs/Au NPs/PRO/HA system exhibited a remarkable increase in presence of HAase, whereas the addition of other species did not exert any obvious influence on ﬂuorescence intensity. Interference experiments were carried out by respectively introduction above-mentioned substances into Au/Ag NCs/Au NPs/PRO/HA/HAase system. It was noticeable that the biosensor exhibits the same fluorescence response to HAase. All above results demonstrated that this developed biosensor possessed excellent selectivity and good anti-interference capability for HAase determination.

Finally, the actual sample experiments were carried out to prove the feasibility of our sensing system to detect HAase. The standard addition method was performed by the introduction of different concentration of HAase into the serum samples and then measured by the proposed method. The results were summed up in Table 1 . The average recoveries of HAase in the human serum samples were in the range of 95.1–101.9% and the RSD (n = 3) was lower than 3.0%, which is in accord with the requirements of practical application.

In summary, a novel bimetallic gold-silver nanoclusters was synthesized by using chondroitin sulfate as the template. The as-prepared Au/Ag NCs exhibited high stability, which had potential practical value for biochemical detection. We further established a “turn-on” fluorescence sensing platform for HAase detection based on the IFE between CS-Au/Ag NCs and Au NPs. By utilizing electrostatic attraction between positively charged PRO and negatively charged Au NPs or HA and enzymatic hydrolysis reaction, HAase can be selective and sensitive detected. The proposed sensing strategy could reduce background noise, which provided a new approach for the wider application of bimetallic nanoclusters in fluorescent biosensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21575048 and No. 21775052), the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No.20180414013GH).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.11.040.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Chembiochem
Nanoscale characterization of gold colloid monolayers: a comparison of four techniques
Anal. Chem.
Novel bimetallic gold-silver nanoclusters with "Synergy"-enhanced fluorescence for cyanide sensing, cell imaging and temperature sensing
Talanta
A facile approach toward preparation of sulfonated multi-walled carbon nanotubes and their dispersibility in various solvents
Colloids Surf. A Physicochem. Eng. Asp.
Sulfated carbon quantum dots as efficient visible-light switchable acid catalysts for room-temperature ring-opening reactions
Angew. Chemie

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qing Liu received her MS degree from Jilin University (China) in 2016. Now, she is currently carrying out her graduate work for her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Prof. Xingguang Su in Jilin University. Her research is mainly focused on the synthesis and functionalization of metal nanoclusters and their application on biological enzyme detection.

Xu Yan received his M.S degree in 2013 from Nanjing Agricultural University. He joined the group of Prof. Xingguang Su at Jilin University and received his PhD. degree in June 2017. Since then, he did postdoctoral work with Prof. Geyu Lu. Currently, his research interests mainly focus on the development of the functional nanomaterials for chem/biosensors.

Qi Lai received his MS degree from Jilin University (China) in 2016. Now, he is currently carrying out his graduate work for his Doctor's degree under the guidance of Prof. Zhiguang Song in Jilin University. His research is mainly focused on the organic synthesis of 1,2,3-triazole complex and their application.

Xingguang Su is a professor at the Department of Analytical Chemistry at the College of Chemistry, Jilin University. She received her MS degree from Jilin University (China) in 1992 and her PhD degree from Jilin University (China) in 1999. Her research focuses on the synthesis, characterization, functionalization and application of quantum dots and quantum dots-tagged microspheres in biomedicine.
